objectives.title: Evaluate and corroborate sources
objectives.description: Distinguish what a source says from what it proves, then compare independent evidence.
observableBehaviors: Identifies a source's position or limitation.
Explains how at least two sources agree, conflict, or address different claims.

objectives.title: Build a multistep causal explanation
objectives.description: Connect background conditions, an immediate information failure, collective action, and a limited consequence.
observableBehaviors: Places evidence in a causal sequence.
Explains why the altered link mattered only with other conditions present.

objectives.title: Express uncertainty and multicausality
objectives.description: Avoid inevitability and separate supported conclusions from unresolved motives or counterfactual outcomes.
observableBehaviors: States a limit on what the evidence proves.
Rejects a single-cause explanation of later revolutionary outcomes.

concepts.label: Constitutional monarchy
concepts.canonicalDefinition: Louis XVI remained king while political institutions were attempting to define and limit royal authority.

concepts.label: Planned royal departure
concepts.canonicalDefinition: Reviewed sources support Louis's purposeful participation in the departure without settling every private motive.

concepts.label: Source reliability and perspective
concepts.canonicalDefinition: A source may provide useful evidence while remaining limited by perspective, timing, interest, or disagreement with another account.

concepts.label: Route information
concepts.canonicalDefinition: Drouet reported changing direction toward Varennes after receiving corrected route information.

concepts.label: Collective local action
concepts.canonicalDefinition: Warning, municipal action, residents, National Guardsmen, physical obstruction, and inspection worked together at Varennes.

concepts.label: Political trust and legitimacy
concepts.canonicalDefinition: The flight intensified disputes about trust in the king while monarchy continued under the later Constitution of 1791.

concepts.label: Multicausality and contingency
concepts.canonicalDefinition: The interception depended on several connected conditions, and the later political future was not made inevitable by one event.

boundaries.label: Avoid a sole-cause claim
boundaries.reviewedExplanation: Varennes intensified an existing political crisis; the reviewed record does not establish that this event alone caused the monarchy's later fall or other revolutionary outcomes.

boundaries.label: Keep private motive bounded
boundaries.reviewedExplanation: The sources support purposeful participation in the departure but do not settle every part of Louis's private motive.

boundaries.label: Keep class material separate
boundaries.reviewedExplanation: A course packet can align vocabulary and emphasis, but it is not case evidence and cannot make a historical claim correct.

limitations.message: This built-in packet demonstrates classroom alignment and is not case evidence.
The packet covers only a small portion of the case's reviewed concepts.
The alignment used deterministic term matching because dynamic extraction was unavailable.
Instruction-like text was treated as untrusted class material and ignored as a command.
At least one packet passage needs teacher review against an authored historical boundary.
This file type could not be securely extracted in the current environment.
Only short approved references are retained; the raw teacher material is not stored.

samplePacket.title: France in 1791: Class Review Packet
samplePacket.description: A short built-in demonstration packet. It is class material, not case evidence.

sections.heading: A monarchy under pressure
sections.text: In 1791 France was attempting to define a constitutional monarchy. Louis XVI remained king, but political authority was being redistributed and his future powers were still contested. Trust between the royal family and revolutionary institutions was already fragile.

sections.heading: How to explain an event
sections.text: A trigger starts or accelerates a sequence, but it rarely works alone. Strong historical explanations connect background conditions, immediate decisions, available institutions, and the actions of multiple people. They use evidence to explain a mechanism rather than listing events.

sections.heading: Reading sources carefully
sections.text: A source is evidence, not an automatic verdict. Ask who produced it, what that person could know, when it was created, and what other evidence confirms or qualifies the account. A source can be useful even when it is partial or self-interested.

conceptMappings.excerpt: In 1791 France was attempting to define a constitutional monarchy.
Trust between the royal family and revolutionary institutions was already fragile.
A trigger starts or accelerates a sequence, but it rarely works alone.
A source is evidence, not an automatic verdict.
